draft.introduction: Isaiah 42 introduces the first Servant Song. The servant brings justice to the nations not through violence but through quiet faithfulness—a bruised reed he will not break.

translation.text: Behold my servant, whom I uphold, my chosen, in whom my soul delights; I have put my Spirit upon him; he will bring forth justice to the nations. He will not cry aloud or lift up his voice in the street; a bruised reed he will not break, and a faintly burning wick he will not quench.
translation.original_text: ἰδοὺ ὁ παῖς μου ἀντιλήμψομαι αὐτοῦ

translation.text: Thus says God, the Lord, who created the heavens and stretched them out: “I am the Lord; I have called you in righteousness; I will take you by the hand and keep you; I will give you as a covenant for the people, a light for the nations.”

translator_notes.note: Matthew 12:18-21 quotes this passage to explain Jesus’ command to silence after healings. The servant’s quiet manner becomes a christological template: messiahship expressed not through triumphalism but through restraint.
